title: Maharashtrian Pav Bhaji

subs.name: 150 g carrots or beetroot, boiled soft; beetroot turns the bhaji a vivid pink

ingredients.name: pav bhaji masala
visual_checks: store-bought MDH or Everest brand; this is the defining spice
ingredients.notes: store-bought MDH or Everest brand; this is the defining spice

subs.name: if pav bhaji masala is unavailable: 1 tbsp garam masala + 0.5 tsp amchur + 0.5 tsp dry mango powder
subs.notes: the flavour will differ but is still excellent

ingredients.name: butter
visual_checks: use generously — butter is essential to the street-food character
ingredients.notes: use generously — butter is essential to the street-food character

subs.name: 2 tbsp oil + 2 tbsp ghee for a dairy alternative that's richer than plain oil

steps.text: Pressure-cook or boil the cauliflower and peas together with a pinch of turmeric until completely soft — about 2 whistles in a pressure cooker. Drain and set aside. The boiled potatoes should already be ready.

Heat 2 tbsp butter in a wide, heavy-bottomed pan or tawa on medium-high. Add the onions and cook until golden, about 6 minutes, then add garlic, ginger and green chilli and fry for another minute. Add the capsicum and cook for 2 minutes until just softened.

Add the tomatoes with a pinch of salt. Cook on medium-high, crushing the tomatoes with a masher as they soften, until the oil rises from the masala and the mixture is dark and jammy — about 8 minutes. Add pav bhaji masala, red chilli powder and remaining turmeric; stir and fry for 1 minute.

Add all the boiled vegetables to the pan. Mash everything together vigorously with a potato masher, incorporating the masala through the vegetables. This is where the dish is built — keep mashing and folding for 3–4 minutes until no chunks remain and the bhaji is a uniform, thick paste.

Add 0.5 cup water to loosen, stir, and simmer for 5 minutes on medium. Stir in 1 tbsp butter, lemon juice, salt and half the coriander leaves. The bhaji should be thick enough to hold its shape on a spoon but still flow slowly. Taste and add more pav bhaji masala if needed.

Slit the pavs horizontally almost all the way through. Heat remaining butter on a tawa and toast the pavs cut-side down for 1–2 minutes until golden and slightly crispy on the cut surfaces. Serve the bhaji in a bowl with a knob of butter on top, remaining coriander, and the toasted pavs and lemon wedges alongside.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cauliflower → 150 g carrots or beetroot, boiled soft; beetroot turns the bhaji a vivid pink; butter → 2 tbsp oil + 2 tbsp ghee for a dairy alternative that's richer than plain oil; curry-powder → if pav bhaji masala is unavailable: 1 tbsp garam masala + 0.5 tsp amchur + 0.5 tsp dry mango powder (the flavour will differ but is still excellent)
